title: MAGF, honestly: why the two-stage decoder plateaued — and the SDF re-engineering that fixes it
summary: Final results from the optimized 2D two-stage MAGF decoder block. The idea (membrane-aware geometry feedback for organelle segmentation) is promising, but the soft-bias implementation turned out causally inert. The failure is diagnostic, not fatal: an oracle membrane sign-gate drives the topological 'leak' to zero at no cost to real organelles — which points to re-engineering MAGF with learned membrane signed-distance fields (SDFs) so containment is licensed by construction. Includes an SDF primer + where this stands vs the recent CVPR 2026 SDF-for-cryo paper.
takeaways: MAGF as built (soft additive boundary/distance attention bias) is causally INERT — ablating it changes predictions by Δ≈0; soft constraints get ignored.
The real failure is a late-training regression: the model leaks organelles into extracellular space, hugging the membrane, and the ECS/boundary scaffold unlearns late.

Diagnostic win: an ORACLE membrane sign-gate removes the leak at zero cost to true organelles — but no post-hoc gate on the model's own sign works, because the sign never learned to flip at the bilayer.

Path forward: re-engineer MAGF with a LEARNED membrane SDF whose SIGN licenses multi-compartment containment by construction — strict where biology is strict (closed bags), permissive where it isn't (open sheets, licensed pores/contacts).

Novelty vs Spatial-SAM (CVPR 2026): they use an SDF as a spatial-consistency memory / boundary cue for SAM2 on 2-class cryo EM; we use the SDF SIGN as a licensed containment gate across a ~48-class organelle hierarchy — an unclaimed intersection.
